data.title: Hermes International reports FY results
data.description:
• Hermes International press release (HESAY): FY consolidated revenue amounted to €16 billion in 2025, up 9% at constant exchange rates and 5.5% at current exchange rates compared to 2024. 
  • Net profit (group share) reached €4.5 billion, up 5.5%, excluding the exceptional contribution on the profits of large companies in France, atthe same pace as sales.

data.title: The market's ironic bull case may be an AI spending timeout: Marko Kolanovic
data.description:
In a twist for the AI trade, markets (SP500) (SPY) (QQQ) could bounce if a major tech player reins in AI infrastructure spending and pivots toward protecting margins and cash generation, Marko Kolanovic, former chief strategist and co-head of Global Research at J. P. Morgan, said on Thursday.

Software (IGV) (XSW) (IGV) has been hit hard this year in a broader tech-led selloff that has been pricing in a mix of AI disruption fears and a separate worry that hyperscalers' spending plans keep rising to potentially unsustainable levels. Meanwhile, a pronounced rotation into value stocks has emerged as one of the defining market themes of 2026

"To rally, ironically, market will need a hyperscaler or a software company to say they will stop investing in AI (stop buying inflated memory) and refocus on cash flow," Kolanovic said in a post on social media platform X.
 
Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META) have released plans to spend a combined $650B on AI this year.

data.title: Bruker outlines 15–17% EPS growth and targets 250–300 bps margin expansion for 2026 amid cost initiatives and market recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bruker Corporation (BRKR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Frank Laukien, Chairman, CEO & President, reported that despite a challenging 2025 with headwinds from academic funding, tariffs, and currencies, "we delivered revenues ahead of our expectations" in Q4. The BSI segment book-to-bill was again over 1.0x, suggesting the company is past the trough in demand seen mid-2025. Laukien highlighted, "we saw a strong free cash flow in Q4 over $200 million after admittedly weaker cash flow earlier in 2025."
  • Laukien explained that ELITech and Chemspeed acquisitions delivered mid- to high single-digit organic revenue growth, while NanoString was flat due to U.S. academic funding pressure. However, "spatial biology, including NanoString, orders were up in the double-digit percentages organically in the fourth quarter of 25% year-over-year."
  • On innovation, Laukien stated, "our innovation engine continued to shine in 2025 with outstanding and very competitive product launches... Many of these recent launches have seen strong initial demand, which we expect to drive revenue growth in fiscal year '26 and beyond."
  • Laukien outlined the 2026 guidance: "reported revenue growth of 4–5%, with 1% to 2% organic revenue growth for the full year and an approximate 1.5% revenue growth contribution from M&A." He added, "we aim for 250 to 300 bps operating profit margin improvement in '26 despite and including a 50 bps currency headwind."
  • Gerald Herman, Executive VP & CFO, provided context: "revenue for the quarter came in about $20 million above our guide expectations. However, despite the top line outperformance our non-GAAP operating margin of 15.7% came in below our expectations by about 100 basis points." Herman highlighted, "we delivered over $100 million in improved working capital performance in the fourth quarter of '25 and with CapEx investments at $22.6 million drove free cash flow of $207.3 million in the fourth quarter of '25 up about $54 million over the fourth quarter of '24."

Outlook

  • The company is guiding for fiscal year 2026 reported revenue of $3.57 billion to $3.60 billion, representing reported growth of 4% to 5%, with organic revenue growth of 1% to 2%, plus acquisitions contributing 1.5%, and a 1.5% estimated currency tailwind.
  • Laukien indicated, "we expect a mid-single-digit organic revenue decline in Q1 of '26, primarily due to the strong Q1 '25 year-over-year comparison. After our first quarter this year, we now expect to resume organic revenue growth in the second quarter and for the remainder of the year."
  • Non-GAAP EPS for fiscal year '26 is guided in the range of $2.10 to $2.15, equating to non-GAAP EPS growth of 15% to 17%. The company expects a currency headwind of approximately 8% to fiscal year '26 EPS, implying constant exchange rate non-GAAP EPS growth of 23% to 25% compared to '25.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin expansion of 250 to 300 basis points is expected, primarily driven by cost-saving initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 reported revenues were $977.2 million, approximately flat year-over-year, including a currency tailwind of 4.1%, M&A growth of 0.8%, and an organic decline of 5.1%.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin was 15.7%, down 240 bps year-over-year. Non-GAAP diluted EPS was $0.59, down from $0.76 in Q4 of '24.
  • For the full year 2025, reported revenues increased by 2.1% to $3.44 billion, with an organic revenue decline of 3.7%. Non-GAAP operating margin was 12.6%, down 280 bps year-over-year.
  • Free cash flow in Q4 was $207.3 million, a record for the company.

Q&A

  • Puneet Souda, Leerink Partners: Asked about margin drivers and cost initiatives. Laukien responded that cost reductions are now expected to yield closer to $140 million or higher annually, and "certainly by Q3, that should be all effective and then become annualized."
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Inquired about confidence in margin expansion. Laukien stated, "we have a lot of confidence in that," attributing it to cost actions and supply chain improvements.
  • Tycho Peterson, Jefferies: Asked about end-market assumptions. Gerald Herman said, "for biopharma, we're not assuming a significant snapback. We're assuming low single digits organic growth. Our semi business... we're expecting to be in the low single digits in growth."
  • Subhalaxmi Nambi, Guggenheim: Asked about book-to-bill and backlog expectations. Laukien replied, "we believe actually that the book-to-bill trends over the last 2 quarters... will continue into this year also aided, of course, by easier comps."
  • Douglas Schenkel, Wolfe Research: Questioned Q1 organic revenue growth guidance, noting potential upside from delayed revenues and pricing. Herman clarified, "not all of that is going to impact in Q1. So I think we are expecting to see some improvement in Q2 from those, but not all of it hits in Q1 of '26."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a slightly negative to neutral tone, probing for clarity on margin drivers, order recovery, and end-market assumptions, with skepticism around Q1 guidance and cost savings timing.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, but was somewhat defensive when addressing Q1 margin and growth queries, using phrases like "we have a lot of confidence in that" and "we now expect these to yield on an annualized basis between closer to $140 million or even higher than that."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone in prepared remarks remains confident, while Q&A interactions reflect more caution and detailed explanations. Analyst tone has shifted from cautious optimism to a more pressing, detail-oriented skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now more specific, with explicit targets for revenue growth, EPS, and margin expansion. The previous quarter emphasized the need to wait for Q4 results before providing such detail.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from recovery and stabilization to active cost reduction and margin expansion, with cost initiatives increased above the previous $120 million target.
  • Analysts’ focus in the current quarter centered on margin expansion credibility, order momentum, and the timing of revenue recovery, while in the previous quarter, questions centered more on order trends and the sustainability of improved bookings.
  • Key metrics now highlight a return to organic growth and robust cost control, compared to prior concerns about persistent headwinds and weak demand.
  • Management’s confidence in cost actions and margin targets is more pronounced, though their responses to analysts indicate greater scrutiny and the need for detailed justification.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges mentioned include lingering uncertainty in the U.S. academic/government markets, foreign exchange headwinds, and the timing of order conversion to revenue.
  • Management's mitigation strategies focus on cost savings exceeding $140 million annually, portfolio innovation, and maintaining high backlog levels.
  • Analysts remain concerned about the credibility of margin expansion, pace of order recovery, and the sustainability of recent improvements in bookings, especially in the U.S. and China.

Final Takeaway

Bruker’s leadership has set clear and ambitious targets for 2026, including 4–5% reported revenue growth, 250–300 basis points of operating margin expansion, and 15–17% non-GAAP EPS growth, supported by cost initiatives expected to surpass $140 million annually. The company enters the year with a robust backlog, strong Q4 free cash flow, and positive momentum in order trends, particularly in spatial biology, diagnostics, and semiconductor metrology. While headwinds remain in academic/government markets and foreign exchange, management expresses confidence in overcoming these challenges, emphasizing operational discipline, innovation, and a return to organic growth in the second quarter and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coincheck Group N.V. reports Q3 results
data.description:
• Coincheck Group N.V. press release (CNCK): Q3 Revenue of $915M.
  • Gross margin decreased 1%, to ¥3,833 million ($24.4 million) in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to ¥3,886 million ($24.8 million) in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, partially offset by an increase in IEO revenue.
  • Verified Accounts increased 2%, to 2,475,345 as of December 31, 2025 from 2,421,080 as of September 30, 2025.
  • Customer Assets decreased 20%, to ¥948.5 billion ($6,049 million) as of December 31, 2025 from ¥1,189.2 billion ($7,584 million) as of September 30, 2025. Even though the quantity of digital tokens held by customers remained relatively stable during the third quarter of fiscal 2026, Customer Assets decreased due primarily to the decline in the market price of crypto assets, including Bitcoin, Ethereum and XRP.
  • Marketplace Trading Volume decreased 7%, to ¥87.7 billion ($559 million) for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 from ¥94.7 billion ($604 million) for the second quarter of fiscal 2026.
  • Net income increased 14% to ¥405 million ($2.6 million) in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to ¥354 million ($2.3 million) in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. The primary driver of this increase was a decrease in income tax expense and an increase in gain from change in fair value of warrant liability in the second quarter of fiscal 2026.
  •

data.title: Kraft Heinz facing growth challenges as a single entity—J.P. Morgan
data.description:
Kraft Heinz’s (KHC) announcement to remain as a single entity eclipsed fourth quarter results Wednesday. But for J.P. Morgan’s Thomas Palmer and Brandon Cohen, the lackluster outlook and risks associated with the cost to drive growth warranted a downgrade to Underweight.

“We see ongoing challenges that could limit KHC’s expected volume inflection as 2026 progresses,” Palmer and Cohen said, adding that if the cost of driving growth for its underperforming segment proves higher than KHC anticipates (which has been the case for General Mills and others in the space), the company might find itself with less flexibility, given that the dividend should absorb nearly 80% of 2026 free cash flow.

The analysts cite North American volume decline of more than 3% in each of the past 19 quarters, despite investments in innovation, marketing, price, and brand renovation.

“The declines have been the result of both weaker category performance (which is less of a headwind lately than in the past) and market share losses (which show little signs of abating),” Palmer and Cohen write.

According to Nielsen data, volumes are trending lower in 13 of KHC’s largest U.S. retail categories, and the company is losing volume share in 10 categories, including packaged lunch meat.

Investments in marketing might be the “right choice” in the longer term, but these investments take time to translate into higher sales. KHC assumes these investments will start to ramp in the second quarter of this year and further into the second half and that volumes will closely follow.

But Palmer and Cohen are skeptical.

“On this basis, we wonder to what extent KHC’s 2026 outlook is conservative,” they say.

data.title: Antero Midstream outlines 8% EBITDA growth and $360M free cash flow target for 2026 following $1.1B HG Midstream acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Antero Midstream Corporation (AM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael Kennedy, President, CEO & Director, announced the closing of the HG Midstream acquisition for $1.1 billion, stating this bolt-on asset in the Marcellus shale "adds over 400 highly economic undeveloped locations dedicated to Antero Midstream that immediately compete for development capital and infrastructure projects in 2026." Kennedy emphasized the strategic fit of the acquisition and reaffirmed the company’s "just-in-time capital investment strategy that generates consistent and repeatable free cash flow."
  • Kennedy reported that EBITDA grew 7% year-over-year in 2025, marking "our 11th consecutive year of growth since our IPO in 2014." Free cash flow after dividends increased by 30%, driven by capital-efficient organic growth and throughput from Antero Resources. He projected continued EBITDA and free cash flow growth in 2026, with expectations for "8% year-over-year EBITDA growth and 11% year-over-year free cash flow growth."
  • Kennedy forecasted "another year of high single-digit EBITDA growth" in 2027 as benefits from the recent acquisition and synergies are realized, particularly from the integration of the water system and AR’s 3-rig new completion crew development program.
  • Justin Agnew, CFO, stated, "Adjusted EBITDA was $285 million during the quarter, which was a 4% increase year-over-year, driven by an increase in gathering and compression volumes." Agnew added, "We generated $85 million of free cash flow after dividends, which we used to reduce leverage to 2.7x and repurchased approximately $48 million of AM shares."
  • Agnew highlighted a company record for the year, noting "free cash flow after dividends of $325 million, which is a 30% increase compared to 2024," and a "20% return on invested capital or ROIC in 2025."
  • Agnew outlined the 2026 capital budget of $190 million to $220 million, allocating funds to well connect and water capital, construction and relocation of compression assets, integration of water systems, and expansion capital on dry gas acreage.

Outlook

  • For 2026, management is forecasting "adjusted EBITDA of over $1.2 billion at the midpoint or an 8% increase year-over-year."
  • Free cash flow after dividends is projected at $360 million, or "an 11% increase compared to 2025."
  • The company expects "further growth in the water business in 2027, as we begin servicing locations on HG acquired acreage."
  • Management reiterated a balanced return of capital program in 2026, stating, "we expect a balanced return of capital program in 2026 in the form of debt reduction and share repurchases. This allows us to maintain a strong balance sheet with leverage in the low 3x range."
  • No comparison to analysts’ estimates provided due to lack of sufficient data.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $285 million, a 4% year-over-year increase.
  • Free cash flow after dividends for the quarter was $85 million, which contributed to reducing leverage to 2.7x and repurchasing about $48 million in shares.
  • For the full year, free cash flow after dividends reached $325 million, a 30% increase from 2024, and the company reported a 20% ROIC.
  • The 2026 capital budget is set between $190 million and $220 million, with planned investments in water and dry gas infrastructure to "unlock significant optionality and improve reliability in the dry gas regime."
  • The company maintained an attractive $0.90 per share dividend.

Q&A

  • John Mackay, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: "I want to start on the growth outlook. I understand '26 and '27 have some tailwinds from M&A in the headline numbers. Could you just walk us through a little bit what the kind of longer-term growth trajectory looks like, let's say, once the assets are kind of fully up and running, if you guys are running a 3-rig and 2 crew program?" Michael Kennedy: "That 3-rig, 2-rig program does provide continued growth even past '27. It's about a couple of hundred million a day of growth on throughput volumes... I think it'd still be in the mid- to high single-digit EBITDA growth like we've experienced over our time, our last 11 years and will have in '25 and '26. I think that's pretty fair to have us generate those type of growth in '27 and beyond."
  • John Mackay, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: "On the AR side, you guys were talking about some growth upside plans... walk us through the thought process there. And then what that means for AM both from an EBITDA growth standpoint, but also a capital standpoint if you move to that higher potential target?" Michael Kennedy: "There's really no capital for AM outside of what we just outlined, Justin did. It's right in the heart of our field. We already have all the big trunk lines. We have whatever pipelines are necessary. We have the water... So really nothing different than these capital budgets that we've experienced over the past couple of years for AM."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability and trajectory of growth post-acquisition, with a neutral tone and specific questions regarding capital intensity and growth outlook beyond 2027.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently highlighting the company's track record and using phrases such as "we expect" and "positions us well."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management retained a similarly constructive and neutral-to-positive tone, with continued focus on growth and capital discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features the closing of the $1.1 billion HG Midstream acquisition, a major development not present in the previous quarter.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with explicit projections for EBITDA and free cash flow growth, compared to the previous quarter's general statements about expanding free cash flow.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from in-basin demand and asset utilization in Q3 to long-term growth trajectory and capital allocation post-acquisition in Q4.
  • Management’s tone is consistently confident, with an increased emphasis on the benefits and synergies of the acquisition in this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the need to successfully integrate acquired assets and achieve expected synergies, particularly in water and dry gas infrastructure.
  • The company highlighted its reliance on "very modest capital budgets" to deliver growth, suggesting a risk if operational or market conditions require higher capital outlays.
  • No new or acute risks were raised by analysts during the Q&A session.

Final Takeaway

Antero Midstream delivered another year of growth, punctuated by the $1.1 billion HG Midstream acquisition, which immediately expands its asset base and sets the stage for continued EBITDA and free cash flow growth in 2026 and beyond. Management projects strong performance with an 8% EBITDA increase and 11% higher free cash flow after dividends for 2026, while maintaining disciplined capital allocation and a balanced return of capital program. The company’s strategy of integrating newly acquired assets and capitalizing on its existing infrastructure underpins its outlook for sustained, high single-digit growth into 2027, reinforcing confidence in its long-term value creation plan.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Conduent outlines accelerated turnaround and $3.2B ACV pipeline while advancing portfolio rationalization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Conduent Incorporated (CNDT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Harsha Agadi, President, CEO & Director, emphasized a decisive turnaround approach, stating, "My expectations are simple and my objectives are clear. It is to lead Conduent to consistent year-over-year revenue and EBITDA growth, supported by very strong and durable free cash flow generation." He highlighted the company's status as a turnaround story and outlined several immediate priorities: faster decision-making, maximum financial discipline, cost structure reductions—particularly in SG&A and technology—portfolio rationalization, improved pipeline conversion, and organizational simplification.
  • Agadi stated the company will categorize every business as "fix, sell or grow," and clarified, "Businesses that are in the category of sale will be actively marketed with a focus on executing transactions efficiently and at fair value. Proceeds will be first used to reduce debt, followed by multiple other priorities."
  • Agadi announced plans to host an Analyst Day in New York City to detail the strategy, priorities, and execution plan.
  • Giles Goodburn, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "We signed $152 million of new business ACV in the quarter, one of our highest quarters in recent years, up 11% versus Q4 2024. Our full year 2025 new business ACV was $517 million, up 6% versus 2024." Goodburn also noted, "Our qualified ACV pipeline remains strong at $3.2 billion, which is up 4% year-over-year."
  • Goodburn stated, "Adjusted revenue for full year 2025 was $3.04 billion, compared to $3.18 billion in 2024, down 4.2%." He added, "Adjusted EBITDA for the year was $164 million, as compared to $124 million in 2024. And our adjusted EBITDA margin of 5.4% is up 150 basis points year-over-year and towards the top end of our guided range."

Outlook

  • Agadi indicated that a more detailed update on strategy and financial guidance will be provided with Q1 results, stating, "We will also be prepared to outline their expected impact on Conduent's financial performance."
  • Goodburn stated, "You can expect a more wholesome update on both these items with our Q1 financial results in early May."
  • No explicit revenue or EPS guidance for 2026 was provided in this quarter's call.

Financial Results

  • New business ACV reached $152 million for the quarter and $517 million for the year, reflecting year-over-year growth.
  • Qualified ACV pipeline stood at $3.2 billion, up 4% year-over-year, with the Government segment's pipeline up 29%.
  • Q4 adjusted revenue growth was reported in both Government (1.8%) and Transportation (1.9%) segments.
  • Full year 2025 adjusted revenue was $3.04 billion, with adjusted EBITDA of $164 million and margin of 5.4% (6.5% in Q4).
  • Segment results included Commercial adjusted revenue of $1.5 billion (down 5.9%), Government $922 million (down 6.3%), and Transportation $609 million (up 3.9%).
  • Adjusted free cash flow was negative $130 million for the year, with Q4 adjusted free cash flow positive $28 million.
  • Net leverage ratio decreased to 2.8 turns.

Q&A

  • Patrick McCann, NOBLE Capital Markets, inquired about metrics for determining which businesses to fix, sell, or grow. Agadi explained, "I'm looking for, does the sector have unbelievable growth metrics?... can we have decent predictable EBITDA margins?... is there a moat around the business?... can the moat be breached with... technology that is extremely dynamic at this time, and obviously driven by AI, GenAI and all of the other variations?"
  • McCann asked about portfolio focus. Agadi responded, "We need to walk away from... let us be everything to everybody... One of the things we as a team are doing is listing out things we will just not do."
  • Gowshihan Sriharan, Singular Research, questioned commercial go-to-market momentum and healthcare depth. Goodburn replied, "We've reshaped the go-to-market strategy and brought the teams closer to the clients... I wouldn't anticipate growth necessarily in 2026, but I'll certainly make the right trajectory as we look forward out into 2027."
  • On healthcare focus, Agadi indicated the strategy is to go deeper in current sectors, "It's not as much as more logos. We have a lot of logos. I think it's going to be getting deeper into certain sectors, where we already have a fair amount of market share."
  • Sriharan asked about AI-driven margin expansion in Government. Agadi noted, "One risk we do have is in the world of AI, some clients may want to take it in-house, but it may not be that simple... we might share some of the savings with the client."
  • Matthew Swope, Baird, raised concerns about AI disruption. Agadi estimated, "15% to 20% of our business may be exposed to it... we're going to need to move quickly... our clients will move quickly."
  • Swope questioned free cash flow targets and divestiture timing. Goodburn said, "You look at government and transportation. And we've done well and got there from a revenue growth standpoint. We've still got work to do in some of the areas." Agadi added, "We are obviously -- we ended '25, as Giles said, negative $130 million... I'm not giving guidance. And I will have guidance. I will have very precise free cash flow goals."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns around portfolio rationalization timing, AI-driven disruption, free cash flow outlook, and the impact of leadership changes, with a neutral-to-slightly negative sentiment, especially regarding the lack of immediate guidance and the challenges in Commercial.
  • Management was confident and assertive in outlining turnaround steps but acknowledged uncertainties and complexity, especially regarding AI threats and the need for speed, as seen in Agadi's "The work is underway... This is a turnaround story."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management’s tone shifted from steady progress to an urgent and transformative approach, while analysts pressed harder for specifics on portfolio actions and financial targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The CEO role transitioned from Clifford Skelton in Q3 to Harsha Agadi in Q4, marking a major leadership change and a new strategic direction focused on turnaround and acceleration.
  • Guidance language shifted from "making good progress" and "on course" to a more urgent focus on speed, accountability, and portfolio rationalization.
  • Analyst questions this quarter were more focused on execution risk, AI disruption, and capital allocation versus prior focus on segment performance and AI opportunities.
  • Commercial segment challenges were consistent quarter-over-quarter, but the new CEO signaled more aggressive actions.
  • Management’s confidence in turnaround and execution was more pronounced, although free cash flow and Commercial recovery remained areas of concern.

Risks and Concerns

  • AI and technology disruption risk was highlighted, with Agadi estimating 15% to 20% of revenue exposed.
  • Analyst concerns centered on free cash flow, Commercial performance, portfolio rationalization timing, and competitive threats from AI-driven entrants.
  • Management is addressing risks through cost reduction, portfolio review, accelerated divestitures, and partnering with AI disruptors to shore up client offerings.

Final Takeaway

Conduent enters 2026 under new leadership with a strategic focus on rapid execution, portfolio rationalization, disciplined capital allocation, and deepening client relationships. The company is committed to returning to sustainable revenue growth, margin expansion, and strong free cash flow, with comprehensive updates and guidance promised for the next quarter. The outlined priorities and structural changes aim to create a nimbler, more accountable organization positioned to address evolving business risks and capture growth opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lincoln Electric targets over $6B in 2030 sales as RISE strategy drives structural transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lincoln Electric Holdings, Inc. (LECO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Steven Hedlund reported "record 2025 performance" with sales increasing 6% to $4.2 billion, attributing growth to acquisitions and price. Hedlund stated, "We maintained last year's record adjusted operating income margin, increased adjusted EPS to a record $9.87 and generated strong cash flows from operations. This resulted in record cash returns to shareholders."
  • Hedlund highlighted disciplined cost management and agility in the supply chain, noting "our savings programs generated an incremental $31 million of permanent savings." He also cited top quartile ROIC and total shareholder return performance.
  • Discussing demand trends, Hedlund noted organic sales grew 2.5% from price, offset by weaker volume performance, with automation volumes particularly challenged but an encouraging backlog expected to drive growth in 2026. "Excluding automation, organic sales would have increased approximately 8%."
  • CFO Gabriel Bruno detailed that fourth quarter sales increased 5.5% to $1.079 billion, driven by "8.9% higher price, 1.9% favorable foreign exchange translation and a 1.1% benefit from acquisitions," but partially offset by 6.4% lower volumes. "Gross profit dollars increased approximately 1% to $374 million and gross profit margin compressed 140 basis points to 34.7%."
  • Bruno added, "SG&A expense decreased approximately $3 million versus the prior year from the benefit of $5 million of permanent savings and lower employee costs, which were partially offset by unfavorable foreign exchange translation and higher discretionary spending."
  • Management unveiled the new RISE strategy, shifting from regional to center-led functions, aiming to structurally align the global organization for greater efficiency and agility, and maintaining a "high single-digit to low double-digit percent sales growth rate framework" through 2030.

Outlook

  • The 2026 operating framework assumes a "sales growth rate in the mid-single-digit percent range with organic sales split 50-50 between volume and the 2025 price actions that carry over to 2026."
  • Volume growth rates are expected to improve starting in the second quarter. Price is expected to be strongest in the first quarter, especially in the Americas Welding segment.
  • The company expects a "mid-20% incremental operating income margin from volume growth and enterprise initiatives, which will result in a modest improvement in our operating margin for the full year."
  • Full year capital spending is targeted at $110 million to $130 million, supporting safety, growth, and productivity projects.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter adjusted operating income increased 4% to $194 million, with adjusted operating income margin at 18%.
  • The effective tax rate for the quarter was 21.2%. Diluted earnings per share were $2.45, and adjusted earnings per share increased 3% to $2.65.
  • Americas Welding sales increased approximately 4%, with adjusted EBIT up 7% to $141 million and margin up to 20%. International Welding sales increased about 7%, adjusted EBIT decreased 4% to $31 million, and margin compressed to 11.8%. Harris Products Group sales rose 11%, with adjusted EBIT up 8% to $23 million.
  • Cash flows from operations were described as solid, aided by lower tax payments, and average operating working capital rose to 17.9%.
  • The company invested $44 million in growth and returned $94 million to shareholders in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Angel Castillo Malpica, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the timeline for incremental margin improvements from the RISE strategy and the impact of M&A and innovation. Hedlund answered, "We have a variety of initiatives in flight in various stages of maturation... I would expect to start to see benefits from the enterprise initiatives flowing in fairly steadily over the course of the 5-year period." Bruno added that M&A returns are targeted at mid-teens percent by year 3, incorporated into the model.
  • Castillo Malpica followed up on near-term organic growth and order trends. Bruno explained the mid-single-digit growth is split 50-50 between price and volume, with automation backlog driving second-half volume. Hedlund added that large project wins were mostly in automotive, but small to midsized fabricator demand has not yet picked up.
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Queried automation backlog and growth expectations. Bruno said automation sales were $870 million in 2025, expecting a "mid-single-digit type of growth trajectory" in 2026. Jones also asked about the shift to center-led functions. Hedlund clarified, "We use the term center-led, not centralized," aiming for standardized yet locally agile processes.
  • Mircea Dobre, Baird: Asked about pricing assumptions amid metal inflation and margin impacts. Bruno stated, "Our price/cost strategy is to be neutral... we have a mechanical adder that's built into our pricing methodology," and metal price impacts are addressed through pricing mechanisms. Dobre also questioned automation margin potential; Hedlund noted the current margin is dilutive, but the target is to get it non-dilutive and then accretive.
  • Steve Barger, KeyBanc: Asked about the industry mix of large automation orders and cyclicality exposure. Hedlund said most wins were automotive, and while the funnel is strong, customer conversion depends on confidence. He added that strategic acquisitions, like Alloy Steel, are aimed at addressing "problems that are pain points for customers."
  • Christopher Dankert, Loop Capital: Inquired about metals volatility in Q1 and international margin expansion. Bruno declined to specify Q1 metal impact but shared the trend is escalating. Hedlund and Bruno said international targets focus on markets with better macro conditions, not on significant European recovery.
  • Walter Liptak, Seaport: Referenced PMI trends and RISE strategy evolution. Hedlund said the company would lag any PMI improvement, with consumable volumes steady but not yet inflecting. He described RISE as a continuation and evolution of the prior strategy.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, recognizing operational execution but pressing on margin sustainability, pricing power, and timing of recovery, especially in automation and international segments.
  • Management projected confidence in prepared remarks, frequently using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we believe," but was more cautious in Q&A, especially regarding automation recovery and external market conditions. Statements such as "We hope... we are hopeful..." and "the caution is really around the external environment" signal a balanced yet optimistic outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a cautious optimism, but management's tone shifted to stronger confidence in structural initiatives and the new RISE strategy, while analysts remained focused on the pace and durability of improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The transition from the Higher Standard Strategy to RISE marks a major strategic shift, emphasizing structural transformation and efficiency.
  • Guidance evolved from maintaining top line and margin assumptions to projecting mid-single-digit sales growth and incremental margin improvements, with a clear timeline for structural benefits.
  • Analysts continued to focus on automation, pricing, and international margins, but questions in this quarter were more forward-looking regarding the impacts of RISE and new targets for 2030.
  • Key financial metric changes included a modest increase in adjusted EPS and margin performance, with continued strong cash flow and shareholder returns.
  • Management's confidence in executing on new strategic initiatives increased, while still acknowledging external uncertainties.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing volatility in trade negotiations, geopolitics, and metal markets as continued risks.
  • Volume growth in equipment and automation remains "choppy" with customer capital investment still lagging, and recovery is dependent on improved business confidence.
  • Pricing volatility in the Harris segment, driven by metals, is managed through mechanical pricing mechanisms but remains a challenge.
  • European demand trends are still a concern for international segment performance, with no significant recovery assumed in targets.
  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the sustainability of automation margin expansion and the company's ability to convert backlog into revenue amid macro uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Lincoln Electric capped 2025 with record results and announced the RISE strategy, targeting high single-digit to low double-digit sales growth and operating margins reaching 19% across the cycle, supported by efficiency initiatives and a center-led organization. The company expects to surpass $6 billion in sales by 2030, with structural transformation, automation, and targeted acquisitions as key growth drivers, while maintaining a focus on disciplined capital allocation and shareholder returns. Management remains cautiously optimistic about end-market recovery and is confident that its new strategic direction will deliver sustainable value and operational resilience.

data.title: Baxter outlines 2026 EPS target of $1.85–$2.05 and signals flat organic sales as company pursues turnaround
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Baxter International Inc. (BAX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Hider reported, "Fourth quarter 2025 global sales from continuing operations totaled $3 billion and increased 8% on a reported basis and 3% on an operational basis. Total company adjusted earnings from continuing operations were $0.44 per diluted share. While the top line exceeded our expectations, adjusted EPS fell short." Hider highlighted margin pressure from an unfavorable mix of sales and nonrecurring items, including inventory adjustments, and cited a higher tax rate as additional headwind.
  • Hider emphasized early turnaround steps: "We are in the early stages of a turnaround and have more work to do to deliver strategically, operationally and commercially and recognize that it will take time to implement real long-term solutions." He pointed to strong 11% growth in Advanced Surgery and consistent performance in Healthcare Systems & Technologies, driven by the launch of Connex 360 and the upcoming Dynamo Series stretcher.
  • The CEO described a new operating model: "We are delayering levers of leadership, including removing the segment management layer and embedding critical functional roles directly in each of our businesses. This will allow each leader to have full P&L responsibility...and importantly, full accountability to the results."
  • Actions to rightsize the IV Solutions business and address pharma supply and backorder issues are underway. Hider also noted, "Free cash flow generation exceeded $450 million in the quarter."
  • Hider stated, "We have streamlined the organization for greater accountability. We have launched GPS to drive continuous improvement, and we have heightened our focus on innovation to better meet customer needs, all to drive improved performance and long-term shareholder value creation."
  • CFO Joel Grade said, "Fourth quarter 2025 global sales from continuing operations totaled $3 billion and increased 8% on a reported basis and 3% on an operational basis. Performance in the quarter reflects growth across all segments. On the bottom line, total company adjusted earnings from continuing operations were $0.44 per share. Results in the quarter reflect unfavorable product and geographic mix, some nonrecurring items, including inventory adjustments and a higher tax rate, partially offset by the positive impact from pricing in select segments."

Outlook

  • Baxter expects 2026 total sales growth to be flat to 1% on a reported basis, with organic sales growth approximately flat. The company projects an adjusted EPS of $1.85 to $2.05 per diluted share for 2026.
  • Grade explained, "For full year 2026, we expect total sales growth to be flat to 1% growth on a reported basis. This reflects current foreign exchange rates, which are expected to contribute approximately 100 basis points to top line growth for the year. Excluding the impact of foreign exchange and MSA revenues, we expect organic sales growth of approximately flat for 2026."
  • Segment expectations for 2026: MPT organic sales flat to slightly up, HST organic sales to grow low single digits, Pharmaceuticals organic sales approximately flat. The company assumes the Novum ship and installation hold will remain for the full year.
  • Baxter guides for a full-year adjusted operating margin of 13% to 14% and a tax rate of 18.5% to 19.5%.

Financial Results

  • Advanced Surgery sales were $328 million, growing 11% in the quarter. Healthcare Systems & Technologies sales reached $827 million, up 4%. Pharmaceuticals segment sales were $668 million, up 2%, while Injectables & Anesthesia declined 9%.
  • MPT's adjusted operating margin was 15.4%; HST margin was 15.2%; Pharmaceuticals margin was 5.8%.
  • Adjusted gross margin from continuing operations was 35.5%, a decrease of 900 basis points year-over-year. Adjusted SG&A was $637 million, or 21.4% of sales.
  • Free cash flow for Q4 was $456 million; full-year free cash flow totaled $438 million.

Q&A

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Asked about ensuring sustainability amid moving parts and forecast visibility. Hider responded, "We are not satisfied with our current performance...it starts with stabilizing the business," citing operational accountability and a focus on innovation. Grade added, "Improving our forecasting accuracy is a major priority...GPS gives us a more disciplined operating rhythm, clear accountability and a lot more continued visibility to the drivers of our performance."
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Inquired about earnings growth post-TSA rolloff. Grade replied, "Do I anticipate growth? Yes. But...the TSAs fall off in the early part of 2027...we are committed to eliminating our stranded costs by the end of 2027, and we remain on track to do that."
  • Marcus also questioned the bridge from Q4 '25 to 2026 margin guidance. Grade described Q1 2026 as the most challenging quarter due to seasonality, Novum uncertainty, absorption headwinds, and pharma margin challenges.
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: Pressed on Novum guidance assumptions and customer communication. Hider confirmed customers can use the device under current instructions and highlighted the alternative Spectrum LVP offering.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked about margin drivers and pharma margins. Grade pointed to a $40 million nonrecurring item impacting Q4 margins and expects improvement in the second half of 2026. Hider said, "We like the fundamentals of this business. We've got some work to do here, and we need to continue to align around the value creation we have for our customers."
  • Travis Steed, BofA Securities: Sought more Q1 2026 modeling detail. Grade reiterated absorption headwinds, seasonality, and pharma challenges as primary drivers.
  • Danielle Antalffy, UBS: Asked about portfolio strengths and opportunities. Hider sees innovation and GPS as foundational, noting smaller portfolio exits are being considered in 2026.
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citi: Questioned guidance philosophy. Grade stated, "I always view guidance...as prudent and reflective of the best and most current information we have available."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly negative to neutral tone, frequently pressing for clarity on Novum, margin outlook, and TSA cost transition. The repeated focus on guidance mechanics and margin trajectory signaled skepticism about near-term improvement.
  • Management's tone was pragmatic and slightly defensive, especially in Q&A. Hider stated, "We are not satisfied with our current performance," and Grade emphasized, "We know we have work to do, and we're attacking it."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's prepared remarks remained measured but with a stronger admission of ongoing challenges. Analyst tone in Q4 was more probing and less constructive than Q3, reflecting caution about the company's ability to meet targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is notably more conservative than 2025, shifting from operational growth expectations of 1%–2% to flat organic growth. Adjusted EPS guidance is lower ($1.85–$2.05 for 2026 vs. $2.35–$2.40 previously for 2025).
  • Strategic focus has shifted to organizational simplification and accountability, with the new operating model replacing the previous segment layer. Innovation, particularly new product launches, remains a core theme.
  • Analysts' focus has intensified on margin headwinds, Novum pump resolution, and cost structure as TSA income declines. Management's confidence appears steady but more qualified, acknowledging the "early stages of a turnaround."
  • Key metrics—such as gross margin and operating income—showed significant decline in Q4, contrasting with more stable results in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Prolonged Novum IQ shipment hold and customer uncertainty present ongoing top-line risk. Hider noted, "Customers are waiting for additional clarity on the nature and timing of the additional corrections that we will look to deploy."
  • Margin pressure from unfavorable sales mix, inventory adjustments, and higher manufacturing costs weighed on results.
  • Continued softness in U.S. IV Solutions and Pharmaceuticals, including supply and backorder issues, remain unresolved.
  • The rolloff of TSA income and stranded cost elimination by 2027 present structural risks to earnings.

Final Takeaway

Baxter’s management underscored the company’s early-stage turnaround, highlighting actions to streamline operations, drive accountability, and invest in innovation. While Advanced Surgery and Healthcare Systems delivered solid growth, persistent margin pressures, flat organic sales guidance, and ongoing Novum IQ pump challenges underscore the work ahead. The outlook for 2026 is cautious, with adjusted EPS targeted at $1.85 to $2.05 and a focus on executing operational improvements and cost controls to support future earnings growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Commerce.com targets $347.5M–$369.5M in 2026 revenue as AI-driven commerce and payments expand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Commerce.com, Inc. (CMRC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Hess stated that 2025 was a pivotal year for Commerce.com with operational improvements and a foundation for sustainable growth. "We delivered revenue of $342 million, up approximately 3% year-over-year, and non-GAAP operating income finished at $28 million with strong improvements to cash generation." Hess highlighted the company's highest sequential improvement in subscription ARR in over 1.5 years in Q4 and noted momentum in B2B, with subscription ARR from BigCommerce B2B Edition customers growing nearly 20% during 2025. The CEO emphasized, "These wins, together with the continued performance of our existing customer base, underscore both the durability of B2B demand and the stickiness of our differentiated B2B capabilities."
  • Hess also pointed to significant product innovation, including the late Q3 launch of Surface, a self-service version of Feedonomics, which showed that "merchants using Surface saw an average 24 points higher GMV growth compared to nonusers." He announced expanded partnerships with OpenAI, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Perplexity, and the upcoming launch of BigCommerce payments in partnership with PayPal around the end of Q1 2026. "We are increasing R&D investment by nearly 30%, focusing on 4 clear priorities that will drive growth."
  • COO & CFO Daniel Lentz reported, "Q4 revenue was $89.5 million, up 3% year-over-year." He stated, "We closed the year with $359 million in ARR and continued strengthening our underlying business fundamentals." Lentz added that operating cash flow was $3 million in Q4 and $27 million for the full year, with a reduction in net debt from $33 million to $11 million year-over-year. He further explained the company will now disclose total GMV and company-wide Net Revenue Retention (NRR), with GMV at nearly $32 billion in 2025 and NRR at 95.2% in Q4.

Outlook

  • Lentz provided guidance for Q1 2026 revenue between $82.5 million and $83.5 million, and non-GAAP operating income between $9.3 million and $10.3 million. For the full year 2026, Commerce expects revenue between $347.5 million and $369.5 million and non-GAAP operating income between $34 million and $53 million. This represents 2% to 8% full year growth with non-GAAP operating margins of 10% to 14% at the revenue guidance midpoint. Lentz stated, "We expect to deliver GAAP profitability for the full year 2026, the first time in Commerce's history."

Financial Results

  • The company reported $89.5 million in Q4 revenue and $342 million in full-year revenue for 2025. Non-GAAP operating income reached $28 million for the year. ARR ended at $359 million, with operating cash flow of $3 million for Q4 and $27 million for the full year. Lentz noted, "Enterprise ARR ended the year at $287 million, Enterprise customer count was 6,648, up 897 accounts sequentially, and Enterprise account ARPA...was $43,200, down 8% sequentially."
  • The company highlighted a shift in reporting by retiring enterprise-specific metrics and introducing GMV and company-wide NRR as key performance indicators. GMV grew 12% in 2025. NRR was reported at 95.2%.

Q&A

  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer: Asked about the agentic commerce landscape and platform opportunities. Hess responded, "We're seeing lots of momentum...We're doing the same across the answer engines, all which have their own intricacies...We're in a good position to take advantage of that, and we'll be testing with Google over the next quarter."
  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer: Inquired about take rate trends and monetization through payment products. Hess said, "Monetization is going to come from a couple of different capacities...Part of that is BigCommerce payment...We've taken a more opinionated approach." Lentz added, "Take rate is not where it can be...We also have an opportunity to better retain and expand the base."
  • David Hynes, Canaccord: Asked about guidance range dispersion. Lentz explained, "The reason we have such a higher dispersion than normal is because we also see a lot more upside this year...We have so much innovation that we have on the road map that's coming this year."
  • Madison Schrage, KeyBanc: Asked about payment offering cadence and margin impact. Lentz said, "We are on track to launch BigCommerce payments roughly around the end of Q1...We do expect it to be additive across the year."
  • Koji Ikeda, BofA: Asked about NRR drivers and guidance assumptions. Lentz stated, "The number for the total business is 95...we do not consider that number acceptable...If you look at just the initiatives that Travis mentioned...almost every single one of them is focused on getting NRR to the number where it should be."
  • Josh Baer, Morgan Stanley: Asked about margin guidance range versus growth. Lentz replied, "Growth is paramount. That is the focus. We are confident...we are going to deliver strong margins as we go through."
  • Brian Peterson, Raymond James: Asked about agentic commerce and replatforming. Hess said, "Certainly, in 2025, it impacted it on the B2C side, most notably, I would say the back half of the year...It's not killed it. We certainly have a healthy pipeline."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on take rates, NRR, monetization models, and guidance assumptions, showing a slightly negative to neutral tone, especially on growth durability and profitability dispersion.
  • Management maintained confidence in new product launches and monetization strategies, but acknowledged challenges: "We do not consider that number acceptable...we have a lot of work to do." The tone was confident in prepared remarks but more defensive and measured in responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' skepticism about growth and profitability guidance increased, while management's tone shifted from optimistic to a more pragmatic acknowledgment of execution risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and operating income guidance moved higher for 2026 versus 2025, with the new focus on GMV and NRR metrics replacing enterprise-specific disclosures. The company accelerated product innovation and expanded R&D investment by nearly 30% compared to the previous quarter.
  • The new reporting structure provides greater transparency but also signals a shift from pure growth metrics to monetization efficiency and platform scale.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from AI product launches and demand environment in Q3 to more probing inquiries about monetization, take rates, and guidance range rationale in Q4.
  • Management's confidence in delivering on innovation and monetization is more tempered, repeatedly emphasizing the need for improved NRR and successful execution of new initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the need to improve net revenue retention and close the gap between GMV growth and top line growth.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about take rate declines, sub-100 NRR, and the wide guidance range for 2026.
  • Lentz highlighted, "We have a lot of work to do. I think that's evident in the numbers as well. But there's a lot of really good opportunities underneath that, that we're excited about."
  • Operational restructuring payments, macroeconomic uncertainty, and execution risk on innovation and monetization initiatives were cited as key challenges.

Final Takeaway

Commerce.com concluded the year with strong operational improvements, highlighted by increased B2B momentum, double-digit GMV growth, and the launch of innovative products and payment solutions. Management outlined a strategy for 2026 centered on driving higher monetization, narrowing the gap between GMV and revenue growth, and achieving GAAP profitability for the first time. The company is focused on executing new AI-driven offerings and payment integrations, while acknowledging work remains to reach best-in-class retention and efficiency. Investors can anticipate ongoing transparency with new key metrics as Commerce.com shifts from foundational transformation to execution and monetization in the coming year.
